strengths: Attempts to link each object to the ethical prompt
Includes some citations and source references
Recognizes moral dimensions of knowledge use
weaknesses: Real-world contexts are minimal or only implied
Links to the prompt are asserted rather than analysed
Lacks explicit engagement with TOK concepts and frameworks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessment places this exhibition firmly in the 1â2 band: three objects are presented but with minimal real-world context; links to the prompt are tenuous and largely descriptive; justifications rely on broad moral claims and repeat the same ideas without deeper TOK analysis. Citations are listed but not integrated, and core TOK terminology (e.g. ways of knowing, credibility, reliability) is absent. In line with the examinerâs total mark of 2 and the rubric descriptors for basic, unconvincing links and superficial justification, a final score of 2 accurately reflects the submissionâs minimal engagement with TOK methodology and evidence.

annotations.comment: The image of the bomb is generic rather than a photograph of a specific preserved artefact. Cite a particular source or archive photograph to strengthen the link to a tangible realâworld object.

The Unit 731 link is a general overview page rather than a specific artefact or document. Specify which report or specimen from Unit 731 you are analysing to anchor it in a clear realâworld context.
The reference to Shams al-MaÊ¿Ärif lacks a clear description of the edition, author credentials or library holding. Pin down an actual copy or manuscript to ground it in a specific context.

The description of the atomic bomb focuses on moral outcomes but lacks discussion of the methods of science or how scientific knowledge was produced and justified. Engage with TOK concepts such as reliability and perspectives.

The account of the Manhattan Project summarises historical motives but does not analyse the distinction between knowledge and information. Develop a TOK argument on whether information about destruction equates to meaningful knowledge.
Citations (Iskander, ICAN, Kempner) are listed but not integrated into the TOK analysis. Use evidence to support specific claims about knowledge production rather than general moral statements.
The concluding sentence reasserts the dual nature of innovation but offers no reflection on how TOK tools (e.g., WOKs) might help audiences evaluate ethical boundaries of knowledge.

The student could clarify how the chosen prompt will be explored through specific TOK concepts (e.g., knowledge frameworks, areas of knowledge) rather than a general moral claim. This will help focus the analysis.

The label âLittle Boy Atomic Bombâ is presented without locating a specific artefact or source. Identify which museum object or historical document is referenced to ground the discussion in a realâworld context.

The introduction states a personal stance but does not outline how knowledge questions or TOK concepts will structure the argument. Define a clear knowledge question to guide the exhibitionâs analysis.
Unit 731 is described historically but without pinpointing a tangible object or record. Specify which document or biological specimen from Unit 731 you are treating as the exhibitionâs artefact.

The argument that âgreater goodâ justifies unethical methods is repeated without exploring TOKâs view on value judgments or ethical frameworks. Deepen the analysis by comparing perspectives (e.g., deontology vs. utilitarianism) as knowledge frameworks.

The moral question âdo ends justify means?â is raised but not analysed in terms of knowledge justification or reliability. Incorporate TOK terminology (e.g., credibility, bias) to frame this dilemma.
The conclusion reaffirms the view on unethical methods but neglects to connect back to the TOK prompt or to evaluate how knowledge is justified ethically. Suggest summarising key TOK insights.
The description of the book lacks a precise edition or location of the artefact. Indicate which manuscript or printed copy is under scrutiny to satisfy the requirement of a realâworld object.

The two viewpoints on black magic remain descriptive; there is no engagement with areas of knowledge or ways of knowing. Ground the ethical debate in a TOK framework (e.g., shared vs. personal knowledge).
The anecdote about Al-Falaki al-Husaynin is interesting but lacks analysis of source reliability, perspective or context. Discuss why this account should be considered credible.

The conclusion pronounces the book unethical but does not explore how this forbidden knowledge challenges standard TOK notions of responsible inquiry. Suggest discussing limits of inquiry in knowledge communities.
The bibliography entries are duplicated under two âWorks Citedâ headings. Consolidate into one section to avoid confusion and ensure completeness.
Citation formats vary significantly (JSTOR link, PMC link, Amazon link). Adopt a consistent style (e.g., MLA or APA) for all entries.
Some sources listed in the works cited are never referenced in the body. Ensure that each citation supports a specific point in the analysis.
The George Iskander reference appears twice with identical URLs. Remove redundancy to streamline the bibliography.
The second listing of the same Scientific American URL repeats without new access details. Consolidate or differentiate the entries clearly.
Long URLs break across lines, making them hard to verify. Consider using DOIs or URL shorteners, and maintain one line per link.
Several sources (e.g., UPENN article) are listed but are not explicitly cited in the commentary. Integrate each reference where it supports a TOK claim.
This page only contains a URL without context or commentary. Ensure that every page contributes to the TOK argument rather than listing isolated links.

strands.content: The exhibition does not reach the standard described by the other levels or does not use one of the IA prompts provided.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of the objects may be implied rather than explicitly stated. Basic links between the objects and the selected IA prompt are made, but the explanation of these links is unconvincing and/or unfocused. There is a superficial justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Reasons for the inclusion of the objects are offered, but these are not supported by appropriate evidence and/or lack relevance to the selected IA prompt. There may be significant repetition across the justifications of the different objects

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of these objects may be vaguely or imprecisely stated. There is some explanation of the links between the three objects and the selected IA prompt. There is some justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Some of the points are supported by evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects and their real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are explained, although this explanation may lack precision and clarity in parts. There is a justification of the contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. Many of the points are supported by appropriate evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition clearly identifies three objects and their specific real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are clearly made and well-explained. There is a strong justification of the particular contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. All, or nearly all, of the points are well-supported by appropriate evidence and explicit references to the selected IA prompt.
